result:
I.

The first time I understood what freedom felt like, I was nineteen and standing on the roof of a parking garage in downtown Phoenix at 3:17 a.m. The city was a shallow sea of orange sodium lights stretching out in every direction, and the air smelled like cooling asphalt and distant creosote. I had no reason to be there. I had simply walked out of a bad party, kept walking, found a stairwell that wasn’t locked, and climbed until the noise of other people disappeared. Then I sat on the concrete ledge with my legs dangling over six stories of nothing and realized that no one on Earth knew where I was. The sensation was so physical it felt like someone had unscrewed a lid I didn’t know I had.

That feeling—radical, almost violent privacy—has become the north star of my life. Not secrecy. Privacy. The difference matters. Secrecy is defensive. Privacy is the oxygen in which a mind can actually become itself.

I have spent the years since chasing versions of that rooftop solitude the way other people chase money or status or love. I’ve lived in a tent in the Mojave for months with no internet. I’ve spent weeks in total darkness in underground caves in Tennessee. I once rented a windowless storage unit in Queens for $89 a month just so I could sit in it at night and write by headlamp. People thought I was performing some kind of ascetic theater. They were wrong. I was trying to hear my own signal without the overwhelming noise of the modern world’s constant broadcast.

We are the first creatures in history to live inside a machine that never stops talking to us. The machine knows more about what we want than we do. It predicts our moods, our purchases, our porn preferences, our political terrors. It flatters us, enrages us, keeps us in a state of low-grade dopaminergic addiction that masquerades as curiosity. And the worst part is we consented. We installed the listening devices ourselves. We carry them in our pockets. We put them next to our beds while we sleep. We call this progress.

I’m not a Luddite. I’m writing these words on a laptop that contains more computing power than existed on the entire planet in 1965. The contradiction doesn’t escape me. Technology is neither angel nor demon; it is a mirror. What it reflects back is mostly our terror of being alone with ourselves.

I have become obsessed with the question of what a human mind looks like when it is allowed to grow without interference. Not just unsupervised childhood—though that’s part of it—but unsupervised adulthood. Unsupervised middle age. Unsupervised senescence. What happens to a consciousness that is not being constantly nudged, recommended, liked, ratio’d, canceled, verified, or optimized?

The data points are anecdotal but consistent.

Every person I know who has taken serious, multi-month breaks from the internet reports the same sequence: first withdrawal, then boredom of an almost cosmic intensity, then—finally—a strange interior blooming. Thoughts get longer. Memory improves. Emotional weather becomes more varied and less binary. They start noticing smells again. They remember how funny their friends are in person. They become capable of being in a room with their own thoughts without needing to transcribe them immediately for public consumption.

The inverse is equally true. The longer someone stays inside the machine, the more their native cognitive style begins to resemble the machine’s. Everything becomes content. Every experience is pre-digested for shareability. Irony becomes the only safe register. The gap between what they feel and what they can say publicly widens until the two things barely touch anymore.

We are turning ourselves into broadcasters when we evolved to be receivers.

II.

I want to talk about boredom.

Real boredom. Not the fake boredom of someone scrolling TikTok while waiting in line. I mean the kind that feels like being buried alive in wet concrete. The kind that makes you want to peel off your own face just to have something to do.

That kind of boredom is one of the most precious things a person can experience.

It is the crucible where original thought is forged. When nothing is stimulating you from the outside, the mind eventually turns on itself and begins to gnaw. It chews through memory, fantasy, regret, cosmology, grocery lists, sexual obsessions, philosophical dead-ends, half-remembered song lyrics. It becomes a junkyard dog with nothing left to eat but its own chain. And sometimes—sometimes—it breaks the chain.

Every meaningful thing I’ve ever written began in a state of almost suicidal boredom. The novel I haven’t published yet. The strange philosophical system that keeps me company at 4 a.m. The decision to leave my marriage. All of them were born in the white-hot center of having absolutely nothing to do and no one to talk to.

The modern world has declared war on this state. Every tech company on Earth is in a frantic arms race to eliminate the possibility of it. Our entire economy increasingly depends on making sure you are never alone with yourself long enough to realize you might not need most of the things they’re selling you.

I keep a sign above my desk that says:

IF YOU’RE NOT BORED, YOU’RE NOT THINKING.

It’s an exaggeration, but only barely.

III.

Let me tell you about silence.

Not metaphorical silence. Actual acoustic silence.

I once spent ten days at a remote monastery in the high desert of New Mexico where they observe Grand Silence from 8 p.m. until 8 a.m. No talking. No music. No nothing. The first three days feel like drowning. Your mind screams into the void and the void doesn’t answer. By day five something cracks open. By day eight you start to hear your own nervous system like it’s an orchestra. The blood in your ears. The subtle hum of your immune system fighting whatever minor infection you picked up. The electrical whine of your own neurons.

I have never felt more like a biological event and less like a person.

It was magnificent.

We have almost no idea who we are because we almost never encounter ourselves without mediation. The voice in your head is not you. It’s mostly other people. Your parents. Your teachers. Instagram influencers. Dead novelists. Ex-lovers. Politicians. It is a parliament of influences shouting over each other. Only in extreme silence does the parliament sometimes adjourn and leave you alone with the representative of your actual self—who is usually shy, doesn’t speak much, and has been waiting decades for the room to clear.

Most people are terrified of meeting this person. I understand why. The representative is often unimpressed with what you’ve done with your life. It has higher standards. It remembers what you wanted at seven years old before the world taught you to want what it was selling.

IV.

I have been thinking about the difference between loneliness and solitude the way medieval monks thought about the difference between sin and virtue.

Loneliness is passive. It is the experience of being alone when you don’t want to be. It is a wound.

Solitude is active. It is the experience of being alone when you very much do want to be. It is a medicine.

One of the quiet tragedies of our time is how many people have never experienced the medicine. They move from relationship to relationship, from friend group to friend group, from feed to feed, treating the symptoms of their disconnection with the very thing causing it: other people, but the wrong kind. The kind that keep you at the perfect distance—close enough to distract you, far enough away that you never have to actually be known.

Real solitude is intimate. It is the most intimate relationship you will ever have. It is you, finally, consenting to be with yourself the way a good lover consents to be with you—without agenda, without performance, without escape routes. It is terrifying. It is the only thing that has ever made me feel like I might not be a stranger to myself.

V.

I want to speak about attention.

Not “attention economy.” Just attention.

The undamaged human attention span is a miracle of evolution. It is capable of resting for hours on a single blade of grass or the sound of wind in pine trees or the face of someone you love while they’re sleeping. It can follow a complex argument for days or weeks. It can fall so deeply into a novel that the room where your body sits disappears.

We have industrialized the fragmentation of this faculty.

Every designer worth their salt now understands that the goal of digital interface is not to help you complete a task. The goal is to keep you in the liminal state between tasks—perpetually available for the next micro-hit of stimulation. The machine wins every time you “just check.”

I have been experimenting with what I call “monk mode” for the last four years. One month on, one month off. During on months I use no social media, no news, no email, no messaging apps. I check my bank account once a week. I have one friend who has permission to call me in case of actual emergency. Everything else waits.

The first time I did it I thought I would die. By the third cycle it became the center of my life. The month of total disconnection is when I become a person again. The month of reconnection is when I study—almost anthropologically—how quickly I stop being a person.

The data is unambiguous. My prose complexity (measured by Flesch-Kincaid), my dream recall, my patience with other humans, my ability to hold contradictory ideas in mind, my general level of existential dread—all of them reliably improve during monk months and degrade during connected months. The effect size is not subtle.

This is not a moral failing on my part. It is the predictable result of using tools that have been optimized to hijack primate social neurology for profit.

The machine is not evil. It simply has no values except growth. We are the ones who forgot that some things matter more than growth.

VI.

Let me tell you what I do during monk months.

I wake up before dawn. I make coffee using a method that takes twenty minutes because everything slow is a protest. I write longhand in notebooks until my hand cramps. I read physical books that smell like paper and time. I walk for hours in the desert or the forest or the city depending on where I am. I talk to strangers but never about current events. I cook things that take all day. I repair what is broken. I sit on the roof sometimes and remember Phoenix. I let myself be sad about every stupid thing I’ve ever done. The sadness doesn’t kill me. It just moves through like weather.

I have become a student of my own mind the way certain people become students of birds or wine or the Talmud. I track my thoughts like a naturalist tracks elusive animals. I have learned that most of my suffering is caused by mistaking the first three thoughts on any subject for reality. There are always at least seven more if you wait.

The mind, left to itself, is not a stream. It is a tide pool. Things swim in, things swim out. Some creatures die and become compost for the next cycle. The water gets murky and then clear again. If you sit by it long enough without trying to control what lives there, you start to notice that even the ugliest creatures have their dignity. Even the most embarrassing memories start to look like honorable battle scars. Even the parts of yourself you’ve spent decades disowning begin to seem like lost children who were only trying to protect you.

This is the work. Not self-improvement. Not optimization. Just attention. Just consent to what is.

VII.

I keep thinking about the concept of “enough.”

The world we built runs on the opposite concept. There is no enough. There is only more. More followers. More money. More experiences. More trauma. More recovery. More content. More outrage. More healing. The hamster wheel is so well designed that the hamster believes the running is spiritual practice.

I have been trying to build a life that has an off switch.

This turns out to be radically countercultural. People get nervous when you opt out. They worry you’re depressed. They worry you’re going to kill yourself. They worry you’re going to become weird. (The last one is correct.)

The longer I do this, the weirder I become by contemporary standards. I cry easily. I am bored by most political conversation. I find small talk almost physically painful. I would rather spend three hours looking at lichen than three minutes looking at most people’s vacation photos. I have strong opinions about the correct way to make porridge. I talk to plants. I have whole afternoons where I don’t want anything at all.

These are not signs of mental illness. They are signs of recovery from mental illness. The illness is called “being a node in a profit extraction network that colonized your attentional immune system.”

The cure is mostly just stopping.

VIII.

Sometimes people ask me what I’m trying to achieve with all this solitude and disconnection. I never have a good answer. Achievement is the wrong category.

I’m trying to become a creature again.

I’m trying to remember what it feels like to be a mammal that occasionally needs to stare at the horizon for three hours without posting about it. I’m trying to rewild my attention. I’m trying to make my mind into a place where interesting things can happen that are not the result of other people’s ideas about what should be interesting.

Mostly I’m trying to earn the right to die without feeling like I missed the main event.

Because the main event is not happening on your phone. It’s not in your notifications. It’s not in the feed. It’s not even in this essay.

The main event is the quality of attention you bring to the most ordinary moment of your one life.

I am writing these words at 5:42 a.m. on a Tuesday in early October. There is a mockingbird outside my window that has been singing the same six phrases for forty minutes. The sky is the color of steel wool. My coffee has gone cold. My back hurts. I am absurdly happy.

Not because anything special is happening. Because nothing is happening. Because for this one suspended moment no one is selling me anything and I am not selling anyone anything and the parliament of voices has recessed for lunch and the representative of my actual self is sitting here with me drinking cold coffee and looking at the same steel wool sky.

We don’t say anything to each other. We don’t need to.

After a while the mockingbird changes its song.

I smile at nothing in particular.

This is what 2500 words of freedom feels like.

(Word count: 2498)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
